Title: Syringyl lignin production in conifers: Proof of concept in a Pine tracheary element system
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: 5-hydroxylates guaiacyl-pathway intermediates en route to sinapyl alcohol biosynthesis, enabling formation of syringyl lignin monomer precursors
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Coniferaldehyde and/or coniferyl alcohol-derived guaiacyl intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 5-hydroxyguaiacyl pathway intermediates leading to sinapyl alcohol
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tracheary element secondary cell wall lignin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Improves lignocellulosic biomass processability, pulping, and pretreatment/saccharification efficiency due to less-condensed lignin with higher beta-ether content
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Transformation with F5H alone was sufficient to generate syringyl lignin units in Pinus radiata tracheary elements, although at lower S/G ratios than cotransformation with COMT
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Good expression throughout tracheary element differentiation in high-S lines, mirroring ubiquitin expression
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Tracheary element differentiation time course in callus culture
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable transformation of P. radiata tracheary element cultures; pyrolysis-GC/MS S/G ratio analysis; qRT-PCR; 2D-NMR (HSQC/HMBC)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F5H expression in P. radiata was sufficient to enable production of syringyl lignin units. 2. F5H-only lines produced S lignin but accumulated relatively high levels of 5-hydroxy pathway intermediate signatures. 3. F5H is therefore the essential introduced step enabling sinapyl alcohol production in this conifer system.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper infers native pine O-methyltransferases can methylate 5-hydroxyguaiacyl intermediates, but inefficiently under increased flux. S/G ratios were higher early during TE differentiation and declined later, suggesting endogenous pathway flux may outpace introduced F5H capacity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Insufficient F5H activity may limit S formation during rapid TE differentiation; incomplete conversion can lead to accumulation of 5-hydroxy intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primary engineering target to introduce syringyl lignin into softwoods; likely most effective when stacked with COMT and possibly other supportive factors such as transport or reductase capacity

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: O-methylates 5-hydroxyguaiacyl pathway intermediates to syringyl-type intermediates in sinapyl alcohol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Sinapaldehyde and/or sinapyl alcohol pathway intermediates leading to syringyl lignin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tracheary element secondary cell wall lignin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Improves lignocellulosic biomass processability, pulping, and pretreatment/saccharification efficiency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Cotransformation with COMT increased S/G ratios up to 2- to 3-fold over F5H alone and reduced accumulation of 5-hydroxyconiferyl alcohol-derived intermediate signatures
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression tracked endogenous lignin-related genes during TE differentiation, consistent with the 4CL promoter
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Tracheary element differentiation time course in callus culture
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable cotransformation with F5H in P. radiata TE cultures; pyrolysis-GC/MS quantification of S/G and 5OH-VG/VS ratios; qRT-PCR; 2D-NMR
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COMT enhanced syringyl lignin formation when combined with F5H. 2. COMT lowered 5-hydroxy pathway intermediate accumulation, indicating endogenous pine methyltransferases were insufficient under engineered flux. 3. COMT therefore improves efficiency of sinapyl alcohol production in pine TEs.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: COMT was not sufficient alone in this paper; its effect was shown in cotransformation with F5H. Reduced 5OH-VG/VS ratios in cotransformants support improved methylation efficiency.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Without adequate COMT activity, 5-hydroxyconiferyl alcohol may accumulate and incorporation of such intermediates could generate benzodioxane units detrimental to pulping efficiency
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable stacking partner with F5H for softwood lignin engineering to improve S-unit incorporation and minimize undesirable intermediate accumulation

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces sinapaldehyde to sinapyl alcohol in the proposed final step of syringyl monolignol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Tracheary element secondary cell wall lignin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Potentially contributes to generation of less-condensed, more processable lignin via sinapyl alcohol supply
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Did not significantly increase S/G ratios beyond F5H/COMT in this pine TE system; contribution to syringyl lignin formation was not established
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Extremely low expression in the analyzed high-S lines despite use of ubiquitin promoter
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Tracheary element differentiation in callus culture
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Stable cotransformation with F5H and COMT; qRT-PCR; statistical comparison of S/G ratios across transformation classes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SAD was introduced to test whether conversion of sinapaldehyde to sinapyl alcohol limits S-unit formation in pine. 2. SAD transcript levels were extremely low in the analyzed lines. 3. No significant difference in S/G ratios was detected between F5H/COMT and F5H/COMT/SAD groups, so a major contribution of SAD was not supported in this system.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although the top two S/G lines carried SAD, statistical analysis and low expression prevented a conclusion that SAD improved SA biosynthesis; authors considered its importance unresolved to unlikely.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Additional transgene burden without clear benefit in this system
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Lower priority target than F5H and COMT for pine syringyl lignin engineering unless expression and activity issues can be resolved
